pending.body: The new PID-by-PID forced-stop cleanup still leaks backend processes when the orchestrator exits in the race window after the TERM grace period but before descendant enumeration/SIGKILL. `src/cli/run.rs:2937-2943` calls `kill_descendant_processes(pid_record.pid)` and then treats `SignalSendOutcome::AlreadyExited` as a successful stop. But both `linux_descendant_pids` / `unix_descendant_pids` (`src/cli/run.rs:2174-2255`) discover children from the current PPID tree, so once the orchestrator has exited its children are already reparented and no longer appear under `root_pid`. In that case `run stop` can still report `process exited before SIGKILL` and rewrite the snapshot as stopped while orphan backend subprocesses continue running. The escalation path needs a fallback that survives parent exit instead of relying only on live PPID traversal at the end.

Partial stale-owner cleanup is still bypassable on the next retry. `reclaim_specific_project_writer_owner` now returns `LeaseCleanupPartialFailure` after `LeaseService::release` reports partial cleanup (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:186-203`), but `acquire_recovery_writer_guard` only attempts that reclaim when `CliWriterLeaseGuard::acquire` fails with `ProjectWriterLockHeld` (`src/cli/run.rs:1777-1815`). Once the first attempt has already released the writer lock, the next `run stop` / `run resume` call acquires a fresh CLI lease immediately and never revisits the stale daemon lease/task. Because `read_project_writer_lease_record` only consults the current writer-lock owner (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:78-85`), the contradictory partial-cleanup state becomes invisible as soon as the lock file is gone.

Legacy `run.pid` records are now treated as live purely on raw PID existence, which breaks stale recovery after PID reuse. For non-authoritative records, `classify_running_snapshot_liveness` returns `LegacyCliProcess` / `LegacyDaemonProcess` whenever `FileSystem::is_pid_running_unchecked(record.pid)` succeeds (`src/cli/run.rs:1566-1572`), and `is_pid_running_unchecked` only checks `kill(pid, 0)` plus zombie state (`src/adapters/fs.rs:387-403`). Older pid files do not carry `proc_start_ticks`, so after the original orchestrator dies and the kernel reuses that PID for an unrelated process, `run status` will keep reporting `running` and both `run resume` and `run stop` will refuse recovery until the unrelated process exits. The updated regression at `tests/cli.rs:5936-5958` codifies this by accepting any live process on the recorded PID as proof that the run is still active.

status_summary: running: final review restart round 22 -> Plan and Implement
